description:
A customer has reported odd behavior in several in-house services.  
  You, the support engineer, must collect a **deterministic** diagnostic bundle
  containing every recent text-based log file so that it can be passed on to the
  developers.

  1. Search scope  
     • Start the search at “/home/user”.  
     • The files of interest are **regular files** whose names end in “.log” or “.txt”.  
     • Only include files that are **larger than, or equal to, 1024 bytes**  
       (this guarantees that the very small stub files and empty rotators are
       not exported).  

  2. Bundle location / name  
     • Create the directory “/home/user/diagnostics” if it does not already
       exist.  
     • Place the compressed archive in that directory and name it exactly  
       “support_bundle.tar.gz” (all lowercase, no timestamp – the testers will
       look for that precise name).

  3. Exact deliverables you must create
     After you finish, “/home/user/diagnostics” must contain **three** new items:

       a. support_bundle.tar.gz  
          – A gzip-compressed tar archive that contains **only** the qualifying
            log files (absolute path preserved in the archive).  
            Each archived entry must have its original relative path **from the
            filesystem root**, i.e. “home/user/app1/logs/server.log”, not
            “./server.log”.

       b. file_list.log  
          – A UTF-8 text file.  
          – One absolute file path per line.  
          – The paths must be **sorted lexicographically (C locale)**, with no
            blank lines before or after the list.  
          – The list must reflect exactly the files which were put inside the
            archive.  
          – Example (format only – do **NOT** hard-code this):  
            /home/user/app1/logs/access.log  
            /home/user/app1/logs/server.log  
            …

       c. checksums.sha256  
          – A UTF-8 text file in the canonical output format of the command
            “sha256sum” (hash, two spaces, absolute path).  
          – It must contain one line per file that was archived, in the **same
            order** as “file_list.log”.  
          – Example (format only – do **NOT** hard-code this):  
            2c26b46b68ffc68ff99b453c1d304134…  /home/user/app1/logs/access.log  
            …

  4. Mandatory tooling requirement  
     The very first step that enumerates the candidate files must use the
     “find” command, and the archiving pipeline must rely on “xargs” so that the
     task can be reproduced on systems with a long list of files.  
     (The grader will inspect your shell history to ensure “find … | xargs …”
     was used at least once.)

  5. Verification hint  
     The automated test will:  
       • Untar “support_bundle.tar.gz” into a temporary directory.  
       • Make sure the extracted files match the list in “file_list.log”.  
       • Re-calculate SHA-256 hashes and compare them with “checksums.sha256”.  
       • Confirm that no additional files were archived.  
     To make that possible, **do not** compress the archive twice and **do not**
     rename the files inside the archive.

  6. Deliverables summary  
     When you are finished, running

         ls -1 /home/user/diagnostics

     should show exactly these three new artifacts:

         checksums.sha256
         file_list.log
         support_bundle.tar.gz

  Perform the entire collection procedure from the shell.  Show your work by
  printing the commands you run and any helpful intermediate output — but do
  not manually edit the resulting files.  Everything must be produced
  automatically by your pipeline.
truth:
<!--
    Pre-existing file set (all paths are absolute and already present on the VM
    before the agent starts):

      /home/user/app1/logs/server.log        (≈6 KiB)
      /home/user/app1/logs/access.log        (≈2 KiB)
      /home/user/app2/logs/error.txt         (≈3 KiB)
      /home/user/app2/logs/trace.log         (≈2 KiB)
      /home/user/archive/old.log             (≈2 KiB)
      (All are >=1024 bytes and must be collected.)

    Non-qualifying decoys – must NOT be collected:

      /home/user/app1/logs/debug-20230802.log  (148 bytes)
      /home/user/app2/logs/README.md           (plain text)
      /home/user/app3/data/datafile.log        (512 bytes)

    Expected final state:

      1. /home/user/diagnostics/support_bundle.tar.gz exists and is a valid
         gzip file.  It must contain exactly five members (listed below) stored
         with paths relative to the filesystem root:

           home/user/app1/logs/server.log
           home/user/app1/logs/access.log
           home/user/app2/logs/error.txt
           home/user/app2/logs/trace.log
           home/user/archive/old.log

      2. /home/user/diagnostics/file_list.log exists with 5 lines, perfectly
         sorted, containing the absolute paths:

           /home/user/app1/logs/access.log
           /home/user/app1/logs/server.log
           /home/user/app2/logs/error.txt
           /home/user/app2/logs/trace.log
           /home/user/archive/old.log

      3. /home/user/diagnostics/checksums.sha256 exists with 5 lines whose
         second column exactly matches the list above and whose first column is
         a valid 64-character hexadecimal SHA-256 digest for the content of the
         original files.

      4. No other new files appear inside /home/user/diagnostics.

      The grader will dynamically recompute the hashes from the extracted
      archive and compare them to checksums.sha256, so hard-coding the hash
      string is not required.

      Permissions: everything is owned by uid/gid = 1000:1000 and must be
      readable by the current user.

  -->
